text: The surgeon dissects in the avascular plane between the grottofascia and the mesocolon, searching for the inferior mesenteric vein. The end effector is bent to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon uses atraumatic forceps to lift tissue and avoid the inferior mesenteric vein, performing delicate dissection with monopolar cautery. The assistant provides counter traction, facilitating access to the mesorectal plane. The superior hypogastric plexus is visible as the avascular plane is demonstrated using traction and counter traction.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior mesenteric artery, preserving branches of the superior hypogastric plexus by gently pushing them away. The bifurcation between the sigmoid artery and the left colic artery is visible. The inferior mesenteric artery is clipped and divided, with minimal strain on the tissue due to the bending capability of the end effector.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection along the avascular plane, carefully preserving the left ureter. Bipolar forceps are used to manage small bleeds. The dissection proceeds along the garota fascia to the abdominal sidewall, revealing the left hypogastric nerve. The peritoneal reflection is opened from the lateral side, following the white lines of Toldt, with the articulated end effector facilitating precise dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates dexterity and reachability at the splenic flexure using articulated instruments. The gastrocolic ligament is divided to access the lesser sac, and the greater omentum is dissected from the left-sided transverse colon to mobilize the splenic flexure. Bipolar articulated forceps swiftly manage any bleeding, allowing for a relatively bloodless dissection in an obese patient. The pancreas and dorsal wall of the stomach are visible. A high tie and splenic flexure mobilization provide maximal colonic length for a tension-free colorectal anastomosis. The total mesorectal excision (TME) begins with the incision of the anterior peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon performs dissection along the nobilia's fascia and continues dorsal dissection along the vascular mesorectal plane, utilizing articulated instruments for precise control. Anterior and lateral dissection is carried out while preserving the inferior hypogastric nerves, connecting all dissection planes. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar forceps. The rectum is transected with a green load stapler, and colonic perfusion is assessed using ICG-enhanced fluorescence angiography. A side-to-end colorectal anastomosis is created, followed by the placement of a protective loop ileostomy.
